title: NIS2 Art.21(2)(j) — Secure emergency communication channel verification
exclusions: Does not test whether emergency communications are intelligible under real crisis conditions, confirm that the communication system meets national CSIRT requirements, or replace tabletop exercises. Only verifies technical availability and MFA posture of emergency communication channels.
evidence: Daily `UC-22.2.46` failure export (CSV), Teams admin audit excerpts with `actor` and `operation`, synthetic probe logs, and signed PDF summary in `audit_evidence` mapping to Art.21(2)(j).

compliance.assurance_rationale: Verifies availability and encryption of emergency communication channels (out-of-band comms, dedicated crisis lines, secure messaging) in addition to MFA, covering both halves of Art.21(2)(j).
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that MFA and secured emergency communication systems are operational.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search verifying emergency communication channel availability and encryption status.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Stop the synthetic probe for `channel_id=WARROOM-Teams` so `last_test_time` ages beyond 30 days while `encrypted` remains true; the search must return `channel_ok=0` for that channel.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Restart the probe so a fresh `comms:test_result` arrives with `status=Success`, `encryption_verified=true`, within the 30-day window; rerun and confirm the channel no longer appears in the failure table.

dataSources: `index=comms` `sourcetype=teams:audit_log` (often via Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Office 365, Splunkbase 4055) exposes `actor`, `operation`, `ResultStatus`, `Workload` for crisis teamworkspaces. Synthetic probe results arrive as `sourcetype=comms:test_result` or `uc_platform:health` with fields `channel_id`, `status`, `encryption_verified`, `latency_ms`. Identity assurance can layer `index=auth` with `sourcetype=OktaIM2:log` or `sourcetype=azure:audit` capturing `mfa_method`. Lookup `emergency_comm_channels.csv` lists `channel_id`, `channel_type`, `owner_team`, `requires_encryption`. ServiceNow ITSM tasks may land in `index=itsm` `sourcetype=snow:task` (Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow, Splunkbase 1928).
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Office 365 (Splunkbase 4055), Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928), Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud (Splunkbase 6553)
spl:
| inputlookup emergency_comm_channels.csv
| join type=outer channel_id [
    search index=comms (sourcetype=teams:audit_log OR sourcetype=comms:test_result OR sourcetype=uc_platform:health) earliest=-30d
    | eval row_status=coalesce(ResultStatus, status, health_state)
    | stats latest(row_status) AS last_status latest(_time) AS last_test_time latest(encryption_verified) AS encrypted BY channel_id
]
| eval days_since_test=round((now()-last_test_time)/86400)
| eval channel_ok=if(last_status IN ("Success","Succeeded") AND encrypted=="true" AND days_since_test<=30, 1, 0)
| append [
    search index=auth (sourcetype=OktaIM2:log OR sourcetype=azure:audit) earliest=-7d
    | where match(app, "(?i)(signal|teams|zulip|mattermost|crisis)")
    | eval mfa_used=if(isnotnull(mfa_method) AND mfa_method!="none", 1, 0)
    | stats count AS auth_events sum(mfa_used) AS with_mfa BY app
    | eval channel_id=app, channel_type="comms_platform", last_status="auth_check", encrypted="n/a", days_since_test=0, channel_ok=if(with_mfa/auth_events>=0.95, 1, 0)
]
| where channel_ok=0
| table channel_id, channel_type, last_status, encrypted, days_since_test, channel_ok
description: Combines scripted channel health probes, Microsoft Teams audit evidence, and identity-provider multi-step sign-in rates to prove crisis comms stay encrypted, tested, and protected with strong authentication during the last rolling month.
value: Stops the compliance story from ending at “MFA exists” by also showing the dedicated emergency collaboration paths were exercised recently and still meet encryption expectations—what incident leaders actually need during ransomware or blackout scenarios.
implementation: (1) Maintain `emergency_comm_channels.csv` with every out-of-band phone bridge, secure chat, and executive war room. (2) Emit synthetic `comms:test_result` events weekly from your monitoring tool (curl/Telegraf acceptable). (3) Ingest Teams admin audit per Splunkbase 4055. (4) Save `UC-22.2.46` daily. (5) Map failures to `snow:task` automation with Splunkbase 1928.
visualization: Table of failing channels, single value count still healthy, bar chart of MFA ratio per app.
knownFalsePositives:
• **Teams admin audit delay** — Distinguish: Microsoft Graph export lags **4 hours**; `last_test_time` stale though operations succeeded. Suppress: require `days_since_test>2` before paging or join Splunkbase **4055** health index.
• **Phone bridges without digital encryption flag** — Distinguish: PSTN path cannot emit `encryption_verified=true`. Suppress: mark `requires_encryption=false` in lookup for voice-only channels.
• **Break-glass accounts exempt from MFA** — Distinguish: ratio drops below 95% intentionally for `app=crisis_admin`. Suppress: whitelist rows in `nis2_comms_mfa_exceptions.csv` with expiry.
• **Purple-team login storms** — Distinguish: synthetic auth bursts during drills skew ratios. Suppress: filter SPL `where NOT [subsearch drill window]` or use time-bound exception register.
• **Signal/Matrix self-host blanks** — Distinguish: sourcetype not yet onboarded; append returns nulls. Suppress: treat null as `channel_ok=unknown` with manual checklist until HEC path live.
equipmentModels: Cisco Webex (admin audit events, meeting encryption status)
Microsoft Teams (audit log via Graph API, crisis team workspaces)
Signal / Mattermost / Zulip (self-hosted secure messaging platforms)
Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Office 365 (Splunkbase 4055, Teams audit ingestion)
Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud (Splunkbase 6553, MFA for comms platforms)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud (Splunkbase app ID 6553) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `comms`, `auth` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `emergency_comm_channels.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('emergency_comm_channels.csv')`.
• **Search-head capacity**: Subsearch-based detections need `subsearch_maxtime` ≥ 60s and `subsearch_maxout` ≥ 50000 in `limits.conf`. For high-volume environments, tune `[search] max_searches_per_cpu` up from default.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.21(2)(j)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that MFA and secured emergency communication systems are operational." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for Okta Identity Cloud (Splunkbase app ID 6553), landing events in `comms`, `auth` under `teams:audit_log`, `comms:test_result`, `uc_platform:health`, `OktaIM2:log`, `azure:audit`. Okta System Log streams at 1000-5000 events/org/hour depending on workforce size; plan ~2-10 GB/month per org.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > Okta Identity Cloud (API token scoped to System Log read). A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[okta_im2_log]
interval = 60
index = okta
sourcetype = OktaIM2:log
okta_account = <named_account>
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `actor.displayName, actor.alternateId, eventType, outcome.result, target{}.displayName, client.ipAddress, client.userAgent, authenticationContext.authenticationProvider`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=comms earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Maintain `emergency_comm_channels.csv` with every out-of-band phone bridge, secure chat, and executive war room. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 2:** Emit synthetic `comms:test_result` events weekly from your monitoring tool (curl/Telegraf acceptable). This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** Ingest Teams admin audit per Splunkbase 4055. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

• **Hint 4:** Save `UC-22.2.46` daily. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** Map failures to `snow:task` automation with Splunkbase 1928. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "NIS2 Art.21(2)(j) — Secure emergency communication channel verification," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the NIS2 control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search correlates `index=comms` (sourcetype=`teams:audit_log`) on the outer side with `index=auth` (sourcetype=`comms:test_result`) on the inner side, joined on `channel_id` across the last 30 days. `type=outer` (left-outer) returns every row from the outer search and fills nulls where the inner search has no match. Use this when the compliance question is 'for every row in A, what does B say?' — the null pattern is itself the finding (e.g. 'ticket opened but no PIR recorded').

Join caveat: Splunk's `| join` subsearch is capped at `max_searches_per_cpu * subsearch_maxout` results (default: 50000). If the inner subsearch exceeds this, rows are silently dropped and the resulting report will under-count. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd log_level=WARN subsearch` for truncation warnings. For inner searches expected to return >10000 rows, rewrite as `| stats` with a larger base search and post-filter, or use `| tstats` if a CIM datamodel covers the data.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.46: NIS2 Art.21(2)(j) — Secure emergency communication channel v]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -30d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.46,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=comms sourcetype=teams:audit_log earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `channel_id`, `channel_type`, `channel_ok`, `days_since_test`, `encrypted`, `last_status`. Run:

```spl
index=comms sourcetype=teams:audit_log earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table channel_id, channel_type, channel_ok, days_since_test, encrypted, last_status
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `days_since_test`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(days_since_test) as p50 perc90(days_since_test) as p90 perc99(days_since_test) as p99 max(days_since_test) as hi
```

The current threshold `30` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.46: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Failing Channels (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Single Value Count Still Healthy (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Bar Chart Of Mfa Ratio Per App (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** The most likely cause is subsearch truncation (see Step 2). Inspect `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=searchprocessor log_level=WARN subsearch` for messages like 'Output result count exceeded 50000.'

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We check that the special hotlines and chat rooms used when things go really wrong still work, still scramble their content when they should, and still get their regular fire-drill test—alongside making sure people actually use the safer sign-in options on those tools.
